text: Newton's laws of motion form the foundation of classical mechanics. The first law states that an object at rest stays at rest unless acted upon by a force. These principles explain how objects move and interact in our everyday world.

The speed of light is approximately 299,792 kilometers per second in a vacuum. This fundamental constant appears in Einstein's famous equation E=mc². Light travels faster than any other known particle or wave.

Quantum mechanics describes the behavior of particles at atomic and subatomic scales. At this level, particles can exist in multiple states simultaneously, a phenomenon called superposition. This theory challenges our intuitive understanding of reality.

Gravity is a force that attracts objects with mass toward each other. General relativity explains gravity as a curvature of spacetime caused by massive objects. This theory successfully predicts the motion of planets and stars.

Thermodynamics studies the relationship between heat, energy, and work. The second law states that entropy in an isolated system always increases over time. This principle explains why some processes occur spontaneously while others do not.

DNA is the molecule that carries genetic instructions for life in all living organisms. The double helix structure of DNA was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953. DNA replication ensures that genetic information is passed to new cells.

Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight into chemical energy. Green plants use chlorophyll to absorb light and produce glucose and oxygen. This process is essential for producing oxygen in Earth's atmosphere.

The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons that communicate through synapses. These neural connections form the basis of thought, memory, and consciousness. Brain plasticity allows the brain to reorganize itself throughout life.

Mitochondria are often called the powerhouse of the cell because they produce energy. They convert nutrients into ATP, which cells use as fuel for various functions. Mitochondria have their own DNA, suggesting they were once independent organisms.

Evolution is the process by which organisms change and adapt over many generations. Natural selection favors traits that improve survival and reproduction in a given environment. Evidence for evolution comes from fossils, genetics, and observed changes in populations.

The periodic table organizes chemical elements by atomic number and chemical properties. Elements in the same column, called groups, share similar chemical behaviors. The table helps scientists predict how elements will react with each other.

Chemical bonds hold atoms together to form molecules and compounds. Covalent bonds involve sharing electrons between atoms, while ionic bonds form through electron transfer. The type of bond determines the properties of the resulting substance.

The pH scale measures how acidic or basic a substance is, ranging from 0 to 14. A pH of 7 is neutral, while values below 7 indicate acidity and above 7 indicate basicity. pH is crucial in many biological and industrial processes.

Oxidation-reduction reactions, or redox reactions, involve the transfer of electrons between substances. The substance that loses electrons is oxidized, while the one that gains electrons is reduced. These reactions power many important processes, including cellular respiration.

Catalysts are substances that speed up chemical reactions without being consumed in the process. Enzymes are biological catalysts that enable life-sustaining chemical reactions to occur quickly. Understanding catalysis is essential for industry and biochemistry.

The Sun is a massive ball of hot plasma held together by its own gravity. Nuclear fusion in the Sun's core converts hydrogen into helium, releasing enormous amounts of energy. The Sun has been shining for approximately 4.6 billion years.

Black holes are regions of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape. They form when massive stars collapse at the end of their lives. The boundary around a black hole is called the event horizon.

The Big Bang theory describes how the universe expanded from an extremely hot and dense initial state. Evidence for this theory includes the cosmic microwave background radiation and the observation that galaxies are moving apart. The universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old.

Exoplanets are planets that orbit stars outside our solar system. Thousands of exoplanets have been discovered since 1995, and some may harbor conditions suitable for life. Detection methods include measuring the wobble in a star's motion and observing dimming as planets pass in front of their host stars.

Stars are classified by their temperature and luminosity on the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram. Main sequence stars like our Sun fuse hydrogen in their cores for billions of years. When stars exhaust their fuel, they become red giants or white dwarfs.

Wave-particle duality reveals that light and matter exhibit properties of both waves and particles. This concept is central to quantum mechanics and explains phenomena like interference and photoelectric effect. The wave nature and particle nature are two aspects of the same reality.

Osmosis is the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane toward areas of higher solute concentration. This process is vital for maintaining cell structure and transporting nutrients in living organisms. Understanding osmosis is essential in medicine and agriculture.

Protein folding is the process by which amino acids arrange themselves into complex three-dimensional structures. The shape of a protein determines its function, and misfolded proteins can cause diseases like Alzheimer's. Chaperone proteins assist in proper protein folding.

Molar mass relates the mass of a substance to the amount of substance in moles. One mole contains approximately 6.022 × 10²³ particles, known as Avogadro's number. Calculating molar mass is essential for stoichiometry and other chemistry calculations.

Polymerization is the process of linking small molecules called monomers into long chains called polymers. Plastics, DNA, and proteins are all polymers with different properties and uses. Understanding polymer chemistry is crucial for material science and engineering.

Galaxies are vast collections of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity. The Milky Way is our home galaxy, containing hundreds of billions of stars. Galaxies are classified as spiral, elliptical, or irregular based on their shape.

The Doppler effect describes how the frequency of light or sound changes based on the motion of the source. Astronomers use the Doppler shift to measure the velocity of distant stars and galaxies. This effect helps determine whether celestial objects are moving toward or away from us.

Electromagnetic radiation includes radio waves, microwaves, visible light, ultraviolet light, X-rays, and gamma rays. These different forms of radiation have different wavelengths and energy levels. The electromagnetic spectrum arranges these radiations in order of increasing frequency.

Enzymes are protein molecules that catalyze biochemical reactions in living cells. Each enzyme is specific to a particular substrate and reaction type, following the lock-and-key model. Temperature, pH, and enzyme concentration all affect the rate of enzymatic reactions.

The combustion reaction occurs when a fuel reacts with oxygen to produce heat and light. Complete combustion produces carbon dioxide and water, while incomplete combustion produces carbon monoxide. Combustion reactions are exothermic and release energy.

Pulsars are rapidly rotating neutron stars that emit beams of radiation from their magnetic poles. They spin hundreds of times per second and can be used as cosmic clocks due to their precision. Pulsars provide important information about stellar death and the structure of spacetime.
